title: Weekly Markets Monitor - Nasty surprises
gist: Easing US-Iran tensions reopened the Strait of Hormuz even as global inflation surprises turned positive for the first time since 2023, keeping hawkish central bank risk on investors' radars despite mixed equities and a stronger dollar.

insights.claim: US-Iran tensions eased following peace talks in the past week, allowing shipping through the Strait of Hormuz to resume.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: Removes a key tail-risk premium from oil and safe-haven flows; watch for unwind of the geopolitical risk bid in gold and oil.
insights.quote: The past week saw US‑Iran tensions ease following peace talks, with shipping through the Strait of Hormuz resuming.
insights.locator: Highlights The past week saw US-Iran tensions ease

insights.claim: Even though the Hormuz closure has passed, its lagged effects are still feeding into inflation and growth expectations, keeping the possibility of more hawkish central banks on investors' radars.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: Investors should not assume the Hormuz episode's market impact is fully over; residual inflation pass-through could still push central banks hawkish.
insights.quote: the lagged effects of the Hormuz closure and firmer global growth expectations have kept the prospect of more hawkish central banks on investors' radars
insights.locator: Global inflation surprises turned positive in May

insights.claim: Citi's global inflation surprise index turned positive in May 2026 for the first time since 2023, led by upside inflation surprises in the US, China and the euro area.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: A regime shift from downside to upside inflation surprises across three major economies simultaneously raises the odds of a less dovish rate path than markets had priced.
insights.quote: Global inflation surprises turned positive in May for the first time since 2023, according to Citi's index, led by upside surprises in the US, China and the euro area
insights.locator: Global inflation surprises turned positive in May

insights.claim: A simple proxy for June inflation surprises is roughly neutral, with 32 downside surprises versus 31 upside surprises, suggesting the May upside shift has not yet clearly extended into June.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: Near-balance in June data points means the May inflation surprise spike could be noise rather than a durable trend; worth confirming with July data before repricing hawkish risk.
insights.quote: a simple proxy for June points to a broadly neutral backdrop (32 downside surprises versus 31 upside surprises)
insights.locator: While a simple proxy for June points to a broadly neutral backdrop

insights.claim: US macro data showed continued economic resilience despite persistent price pressures, while activity improved modestly in the eurozone, strengthened in Japan, and eased (though remained robust) in India.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: Growth momentum is diverging across major economies (Japan strengthening, India cooling from a high base), which complicates a uniform global monetary policy narrative.
insights.quote: macro data pointed to continued resilience in the US despite persistent price pressures; activity improved modestly in the eurozone, strengthened in Japan, and eased in India while remaining robust
insights.locator: Highlights The past week saw US-Iran tensions ease

insights.claim: China held policy rates steady this week and instead introduced measures aimed at improving liquidity management and policy transmission, rather than easing headline rates further.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: China is signaling a preference for targeted liquidity tools over blunt rate cuts, implying policymakers see transmission mechanisms, not the cost of capital, as the binding constraint.
insights.quote: China kept policy rates steady and introduced measures to improve liquidity management and policy transmission.
insights.locator: Highlights The past week saw US-Iran tensions ease

insights.claim: Major global stock markets closed the week mixed while US Treasury yields declined, the dollar strengthened, and oil prices eased, a combination consistent with fading geopolitical risk premium alongside sticky hawkish-tilt inflation data.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: A stronger dollar plus falling yields alongside easing oil is an unusual mix worth monitoring for gold, since dollar strength is typically a headwind while falling real yields are typically a tailwind.
insights.quote: Major global stock markets closed the week mixed, while US Treasury yields declined, the dollar strengthened and oil prices eased.
insights.locator: Major global stock markets closed the week mixed
